text: The goal of this video is to provide a consistent and standardized operating technique which is reproducible, which provides recognizable anatomical patterns for trainees, and which leads to a predictable clinical outcome.
A standardized approach for a left medial to lateral dissection and splenic flexure mobilization using the da Vinci XI system is going to be described step by step in the following order.

The surgeon places 8mm and 12mm ports in a straight line parallel to the recto sigmoidal junction, starting two finger breadths from the superior anterior iliac spine, with 7cm spacing between them. An additional 12mm assistant port is positioned in triangulation between the R-three and R-four ports.
The operating table is tilted into a Trendelenburg position with a right tilt to optimize surgical access to the recto sigmoidal junction.
The surgeon positions the omentum above the transverse colon and gently moves the small bowel to the right hemiabdomen, revealing the trides ligament for identification.
The robotic arms are docked, and the operator inputs the target anatomy, focusing on the recto sigmoidal junction, into the robotic system for precise surgical navigation.
The surgeon initiates the dissection by making an incision in the peritoneum, creating a broad opening approximately two centimeters above the sacral promontory.
The pneumoperitoneum inflates behind the incised peritoneum, aiding in the identification of dissection planes by tracking along the posterior side of the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon carefully navigates the dissection plane to avoid damaging the hypogastric nerve and the left common iliac artery and vein, which are visibly close to the dissection area, particularly in slim patients.

The surgeon dissects around the inferior mesenteric vein, carefully identifying the embryological plane connecting it to the inferior mesenteric artery, while noting the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum beneath the inferior mesenteric vein, allowing insufflated air to dissect the tissues and reveal the correct embryological planes.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the inferior mesenteric artery plane, carefully navigating around the nearby retroperitoneal structures, including the gonadal vessels, ureter, and hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon reaches the inferior mesenteric artery and creates a natural window inferior to the left colic branch to prepare for ligation of the artery.
The surgeon places three Hem-o-lok clips under the junction of the left colic artery to achieve ligation, ensuring secure closure of the vessel.
The surgeon carefully prepares around the inferior mesenteric vein, ensuring no injury to the nearby duodenum. Hemlock clips are applied to the vein, similar to the technique used for the artery.
The surgeon continues medial to lateral mobilization, carefully identifying and dissecting the pancreas away from the mesocolon to avoid inadvertently lifting and dissecting beneath it.

The surgeon applies traction and caudal traction medially to identify Toldt's fascia, performing meticulous dissection to develop the embryological planes. Small capillaries that end abruptly serve as a visual guide to the correct dissection planes.
The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the descending colon, having fully separated the mesocolon from Toldt's fascia.

A swab is positioned over the pancreas to protect the organ and serve as a marker during lateral dissection. The surgeon continues lateral dissection, following the previously established plane from the medial side, until reaching the swab above the pancreas.
The surgeon dissects the lienocolic and phrenocolic ligaments at the splenic flexure, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins separating the omentum from its colonic attachments to access the lesser sac, carefully lifting the omentum towards the spleen to prevent injury to the splenic capsule.

The assistant provides counter traction by pulling the colon inferiorly, while the surgeon carefully distinguishes between the fatty tissues of the omentum and mesocolon to identify the correct plane leading to the lesser sac.

The surgeon uses the previously placed swab over the pancreas as a marker to complete the mobilization of the splenic flexure. The dissection progresses by separating the omental attachments from the transverse colon, allowing entry into the lesser sac. Remaining attachments are liberated, resulting in the complete freeing of the splenic flexure.
Thank you.
